insight: When Xaetron is in a set with a 5-health absorber like Energy Matrix, it is better to click Xaetron every turn rather than alternating absorb between Xaetron and Matrix. Alternating absorb yields only ~1 bonus battle value per turn, while clicking Xaetron when possible yields ~2.85 BV per turn. Even absorbing once every 3 turns only yields ~2.33 BV per turn, making the clicking line clearly superior.
author: leary93
context: Discussion about optimal Xaetron usage in a set with Energy Matrix, prompted by a replay review.

insight: When Xaetron is paired with only Wall as the available absorber, alternating absorb on Xaetron every 2 turns gives ~3 BV absorb vs ~2 BV without alternating, but alternating every 3 turns gives only ~3.33 BV - not enough gain over the 2-turn alternate to justify the additional inflation cost of overdefending more frequently.
author: leary93
context: Continuation of Xaetron absorb optimization discussion, extending the analysis to Wall-only absorb scenarios.

insight: Antima Comet and Wincer are a strong combo; the recommended build line is DD - DD - DDB - SCD - AB - Wincer, then follow up with Antima Comet if allowed.
author: leary93
context: Analysis of an inefficient line of play, with leary93 suggesting a cleaner build sequence involving Wincer into Antima Comet.

insight: In sets where Thunderhead is present, delaying or not getting Thunderhead is worse than committing to it on turn 5. Getting T5 Thunderhead into Oxide Animus then DD allows easy Cynestra production. Being afraid to lose 2 Engineers to a timing attack is often an overcorrection.
author: leary93
context: Replay review discussing P2's failure to mirror P1's T6 Thunderhead line with an earlier T5 Thunderhead of their own.

insight: An Animus that produces enough red to buy 10 Tarsiers is considered a decent investment when Tarsier is the best attacker in the set, even if it means eventually exhausting supply and having to transition to a second Blastforge for further attack.
author: mrguy888
context: Player asked whether going all-in on Tarsiers was correct when Tarsier was the best attacker in the set.

insight: Attacking with 1-lifespan Grimbotches when taking damage is almost never correct; defending with them is worth roughly 2/r damage worth of soak per unit, far exceeding the 1 damage they deal by attacking. The exception is when breaching or exploiting an opponent's vulnerability.
author: namington
context: Beginner replay review discussing the Grimbotch/Lancetooth interaction and proper Grimbotch usage.

insight: Lancetooths work best in higher-economy sets, but their presence in the set makes the set lower-economy overall (since both players must answer the lancetooth threat), creating a paradox where they force low-econ games even though they are most effective in high-econ ones.
author: namington
context: Analysis of a set with Grimbotch and Lancetooth for a beginner, discussing strategic implications of Lancetooth presence.

insight: In highly tactical sets (aggressive sets with little efficient defense), buying a Rhino to absorb 1 health per turn saves roughly 2 gold equivalent per turn when factoring in inflation, making it more valuable than green tech purchases like Conduit in those matchups.
author: namington
context: Beginner replay review explaining why buying Rhino for absorb was better than buying a Conduit for Forcefields in a tactical set.

insight: 'Inflation' in Prismata refers to the compounding return multiplier of a unit per turn: a Drone bought for 3 gold that clicks for 1 gold has inflation of 4/3 ≈ 1.333. This is the factor by which the economy grows each turn if that unit is the most efficient purchase, and it governs when it is correct to transition from economy to attack.
author: namington
context: Extended explanation of the 'inflation' concept for a new player asking about strategic analysis methodology.

insight: Fission Turret is a highly efficient attacker: without using its click, it is approximately 115% efficient at drone inflation and 105% efficient at Tarsier inflation according to Yujiri's mathematical framework, making it one of the stronger attackers in the game.
author: namington
context: Discussion of unit inflation values and efficiency among expert players, referencing Yujiri's mathematical analysis.

insight: Bloodrager is considered by some experts to be the highest-inflation unit in an average game, because it is extremely efficient even when it only denies 2 absorb. Tatsu Nullifier and Vai Mauronax are higher only when getting full chill value, which is not guaranteed.
author: namington
context: Expert discussion about which units have the highest inflation values in typical game conditions.

insight: Deadeye Operative: if attack in the set is bad (few or no good attackers), buy all 4 Deadeyes. If attack is mediocre, buying 1-2 Deadeyes can work as a supplementary strategy, such as when going double blue for Energy Matrix without wanting to buy double Steelsplitters.
author: crash_overlord
context: amalloy asked how to evaluate Deadeye Operative and how many to build; crash_overlord provided a concise heuristic.

insight: Arka (Vai Mauronax) is extremely efficient, but a second Arka directly counters the first one if total attack is below 14: when both players have ~8 attack and both buy Arka, the second player spends only ~2 attack on their Arka while the first player effectively spends 7, making mirror Arka purchases highly asymmetric in value at low attack totals.
author: namington
context: Beginner replay review explaining the strategic dynamics of Arka (Vai Mauronax) and when a mirror purchase counters the first buyer.

insight: Venge Cannon strategy: play as greedy as possible in the econ phase, then convert all Venge Cannons simultaneously once the opponent starts building attack, and use spare gold to spam Conduits.
author: leary93
context: New player asked about the general strategy for using Venge Cannons; leary93 provided a concise strategic outline.

insight: Centrifuge is only worth buying early (e.g. turn 2) if there are high-impact units you want to rush that you cannot otherwise support the tech for. Vai Mauronax and Odin both qualify if the set lacks good red/blue sinks, but Luci does not because she makes RRRR easy to spend anyway and does not provide immediate threat comparable to Vai or Odin, while also being much more breach-vulnerable.
author: xujhan
context: Shadourow asked a broad set of questions about multiple units; xujhan provided a focused answer on Centrifuge timing.

insight: Endotherm Kit is only worth getting if inflation is low, you are already planning to be in those colors, and either chill is unusually good (e.g. lifespan defenders, or the opponent is very breach-vulnerable) or absorb in the set is unusually low.
author: xujhan
context: Shadourow asked for tips on several units including Endotherm Kit; xujhan gave a concise evaluation framework.

insight: Thorium Dynamo provides such efficient openers that building 3 is typically correct; fewer might be considered only in very fast sets where aggressive attack units dominate. If green is useless, 3 Dynamos is a good number for higher-economy games.
author: consensus
context: Shadourow asked about optimal Thorium Dynamo count; xujhan and f0x both contributed opinions pointing toward 3 as the standard number.
